description:
# Make declared @spec narrow downstream callers and gate reduction

## Goal

`fz-ul4.31` shipped @spec parsing + validation
(`spec_check::validate_specs` enforces `inferred ⊆ declared`).
What's missing:

1. **Downstream callers see declared return.** When the typer types
   a callsite `g(x)` and `g` has a declared `@spec`, the inferred
   return Descr at that callsite is the declared return — not a
   walk of `g`'s body.
2. **Reducer treats @spec'd fns as boundaries by default.** The
   user opted into emitting `g` as a body by declaring its spec;
   downstream callers see the narrow declared return without
   triggering reduction into `g`.

## What this buys

- A library author writes `@spec` once. Downstream callers reason
  about the function via the spec, not the body. Mirrors the
  user-facing contract: annotations are firewalls.
- Compile time: reducer doesn't recurse into `@spec`'d bodies.
- Code shape: `@spec`'d fns get one body each (the declared shape),
  not per-callsite specializations.

## Acceptance

- A fixture demonstrating: a function `f` with `@spec :: int → int`,
  called from main with `f(42)` and `f(some_runtime_int)`. After
  this ticket, `f` has 1 body; both callers see return type `int`.
- `fixtures/spec_ok` passes unchanged (it already declares `@spec`).
- `spec_check::validate_specs` still passes for every fixture (the
  declared bound is still a bound).

## Removals

None directly. Reducer logic gains an early-stop branch for @spec'd
callees.

## Future

- Typed receive (`fz-ul4.19`) extends this story: a typed mailbox
  is a boundary annotation with the same shape.
- Typed externs already declare return Descrs; reducer can already
  use them.
title: fz-RED.9: @spec as downstream-narrowing contract
